name: chile colorado
instructions:
MEAT IS REQUIRED — TWO PATHS. Chile colorado cannot be a bean-only chili. Pick one path:
- CHUNKY BEEF PATH: real beef (chuck or similar), cut into bite-sized chunks. NO BEANS alongside.
- GROUND MEAT PATH: ground beef, ground turkey, or a mix. Add 1 can of pinto beans alongside.

CHILE MIX: rehydrate a mix of dried chiles. Canonical starting ratio: 2 parts new mexico + 1 part ancho + 1 part guajillo. New mexico can carry the dish alone if that's what you have; more new mexico is safer than more guajillo or ancho. Pasilla, california chiles also welcome — all dried chiles are OK here. New mexico chili powder is the reliable dry-form workhorse; keep it as the seasoning-side chile.

ONION + CHILE PREP: process the white onions to a fine dice or paste (food processor works, or sauté-then-blend). Add garlic and jalapeños to the prep. Fry the resulting paste in LARD AND VEGETABLE OIL. Lard carries the character; vegetable oil supplies volume of fat and keeps the lard from burning.

SEASON: dried oregano and ground cumin are the primary dry seasonings on top of the chile work. Rosemary and thyme are secondary — welcome if you want them, not required.

SEAR AND BUILD: sear the meat in the chile paste. Deglaze with BEEF STOCK (primary — chicken stock or vegetable stock secondary). Add tomato paste OR a splash of worcestershire sauce (one or the other — see notes on the depth requirement). Simmer until the chunks are tender (chunky beef path) or the ground meat is cooked through and the beans are integrated (ground meat path).

BUILD BODY WITH MASA HARINA: add masa harina near the end to thicken. The liquid should largely cook off — the goal is a thick red sauce coating meat (and beans, if using), not a soupy stew.

CORRECT FOR FRUITINESS IF NEEDED: if the sauce reads fruity/candied (from too much guajillo or too much tomato paste), stir in a microdose of unsweetened chocolate. This is the fruitiness correction step (see notes).

FINISH: dollop of sour cream + thinly-sliced scallions on top. Salt to taste throughout.
notes:
INTRO: this recipe aligns with classical chile colorado more than earlier versions did. Meat is required (chunky beef or ground); the sauce coats it. Onions and garlic are heavily processed (fine dice or blended). Beef stock is primary; either tomato paste or worcestershire supplies depth; a chile mix with new mexico dominant carries the flavor; masa harina builds body.

IT'S A SAUCE, NOT A STEW: chile colorado is supposed to complement the meat, not stand on its own. The sauce coats; it doesn't become a soup. Trying to push it into a stew-like body (more stock, more bouillon, more chili powder) usually fails — you can't rescale this dish that way without ruining it.

MEAT IS REQUIRED — TWO PATHS ONLY:
- CHUNKY BEEF PATH: real beef in bite-sized chunks, cooked in the sauce until tender. NO BEANS in this path — the chunks carry the whole texture and beans would compete.
- GROUND MEAT PATH: ground beef and/or ground turkey, plus 1 can of pinto beans. The ground meat's smaller texture leaves room for the beans to add structural bite. Beans without ground meat is not a real path here — the dish reads flat.
No bean-only version. Chile colorado without meat is a different (and worse) dish.

THE TWO PATHS WANT DIFFERENT SAUCE CHARACTER:
- CHUNKY BEEF PATH → SAUCE CAN BE PUNGENT AND SPICY: the sauce is a medium to eat the meat out of — the chunks are the meal, the sauce coats. That lets the sauce lean assertive: more jalapeños, more new mexico chili powder, more chile heat overall, more depth. You're not going to drink the sauce; you're using it to season each bite of beef. Push the flavor.
- GROUND MEAT + BEAN PATH → MORE OF A STEW: the ground meat and pinto beans distribute through the sauce, and you end up eating sauce, meat, and beans together in every spoonful. That makes the whole dish read more like a stew, and the sauce needs to be pleasant to eat in volume. Dial the heat and pungency back a notch — cumin/oregano/masa harina/garlic still lead, but jalapeños and chili powder are moderated. The sauce becomes something you actually taste on its own rather than just a seasoning medium.
Same recipe, two sauce personalities depending on which path you're on.

WHITE ONIONS: white onions are the right onion here, not yellow. Sharper, cleaner background against the chiles. Yellow onions soften what should read crisp.

ONION + CHILE PREP — TWO PATHS, BOTH FINE:
- FOOD PROCESSOR DICE: finely dice the raw white onions in a food processor before adding to the pan.
- SAUTE THEN BLEND: sauté the white onions (and garlic, jalapeños) first, then blend with the rehydrated chiles.
Either produces the fine, smooth onion-in-sauce result the dish wants. Both keep you OUT of american-chili territory (which is what chunky diced onions in a tomato base would do).

DRIED CHILES — NEW MEXICO DOMINANT, ALL OTHERS WELCOME: the previous rule (guajillo + new mexico as an equal foundational pair; ancho and pasilla clashing) is out. New framing:
- ALL DRIED MEXICAN CHILES ARE WELCOME: guajillo, ancho, pasilla, california, new mexico. Mix freely.
- CANONICAL STARTING RATIO: 2 parts new mexico + 1 part ancho + 1 part guajillo. Add pasilla or california if you have them.
- NEW MEXICO ALONE CAN CARRY THE DISH: if you only have new mexico chiles or new mexico chili powder, the dish still works. More new mexico is always safer than more guajillo or ancho.
- LESS OF GUAJILLO / ANCHO / PASILLA THAN NEW MEXICO: guajillo brings fruitiness that can drift the sauce candied if overdone; ancho and pasilla bring dark bitter character that can push toward mole territory. Keep them present but subordinate to new mexico. New mexico brings the smoky-earthy depth that anchors the dish.
- CHOCOLATE IS THE CORRECTION FOR TOO MUCH GUAJILLO: if you tilt guajillo-heavy (or use a lot of tomato paste) and the sauce reads fruity, chocolate balances it (see chocolate note below).

DEPTH REQUIREMENT — TOMATO PASTE OR WORCESTERSHIRE, ONE OF THE TWO: chile colorado needs a depth component. Two paths, either works:
- TOMATO PASTE: a modest spoonful, cooked into the chile paste as it fries. Adds sweet/umami backbone. This is the more traditional-mexican path.
- WORCESTERSHIRE SAUCE: a small splash. Adds tamarind-and-umami depth — the tamarind in worcestershire hits the same slot as tomato paste but from a different angle. This is the more european-adjacent path.
Use ONE or the other; using both in meaningful quantity crowds the flavor. Without either, the sauce reads flat.

DON'T UNDERESTIMATE MASA HARINA, JALAPEÑOS, AND GARLIC FOR FLAVOR-BUILDING: when the sauce reads bland, the move is NOT more chili powder or more stock. Those usually make it worse. The correct move is more masa harina (body and corn depth), more jalapeños (vegetal sharpness and savory backbone), and more garlic.

HERB/SPICE HIERARCHY — CUMIN + OREGANO PRIMARY; ROSEMARY + THYME SECONDARY: dried oregano and ground cumin are the canonical dry seasonings on top of the chile work. Rosemary and thyme both work in a supporting role — they add a european-stew register that's welcome in moderation but not required. This is a change from the previous stance that thyme was clashing; on further testing thyme fits fine when dosed lightly, especially in the chunky-beef path where the dish leans more european-beef-stew-adjacent. Cumin and oregano still lead.

ADOBO IS CLASHING — CHIPOTLE-IN-ADOBO BELONGS ELSEWHERE: this dish is dried-chile-forward, not adobo-forward. Chipotle chiles in adobo belong in master mexican black beans, mole verde, and other adobo-shaped dishes. Adding them here muddies the bright dried-chile profile with smoky-tangy notes meant for different recipes. Same principle as the cilantro rule: cilantro and adobo go together; chile colorado wants neither.

CILANTRO IS CLASHING HERE — IT BELONGS IN ADOBO-FORWARD MEXICAN DISHES: cilantro reads great in adobo-forward dishes. It does NOT belong in chile colorado. The dried-chile + masa-harina + lard register wants the scallion-and-jalapeño green note, not cilantro's herbal-citrus.

BAY LEAF AND CINNAMON — PRIMARY BUT OPTIONAL FLAVOR ADDITIVES: both bay leaf and cinnamon (a stick or a small pinch of ground) belong in the primary ingredient list — they're canonical additions to chile colorado — but they're OPTIONAL, not required. The dried-chile + masa-harina + lard base is already dense enough that the dish works without them; adding them lifts and rounds the sauce with a background warm-spice complexity that's characteristically mexican. Cinnamon in particular pairs well with the chocolate (both are dark warm notes) and helps damp any residual guajillo fruitiness alongside chocolate's bitterness. Bay leaf sits in the european-braise register but reads more integrated here than it did in the old bay-leaf-clashing framing. Use small amounts of both; heavy hands push toward mole territory.

JALAPEÑO: not really there for heat — it's the savory backbone the sauce otherwise lacks. Also useful vegetally against the dried-chile paste. Anaheim peppers can ride alongside as a milder fresh-pepper note.

GREEN ONIONS AND JALAPEÑOS COMPLEMENT EACH OTHER HERE: scallions and jalapeños make a good pair — jalapeños go deep into the cooked sauce, scallions go on top raw. Together they bracket the green-vegetal axis without either taking over.

CHICKEN: chicken doesn't belong in the final dish. Chicken thighs CAN be cooked in the broth briefly to flavor the sauce, then removed and served as a side coated in the red sauce. Don't leave chicken in the final pot.

DAIRY FINISH — SOUR CREAM WITH SCALLIONS, NOT CHEESE: cool tangy dairy cuts the chile heat; scallions add sharp fresh-onion brightness that complements the jalapeño backbone. Cheese (cheddar, monterrey jack) is an american-chili move and stays as a secondary option; the canonical finish is sour cream + scallions.

LARD + VEGETABLE OIL — BOTH, NOT ONE OR THE OTHER: lard carries the character that neutral oils can't — depth and roundness that integrates with the dried-chile and masa-harina register. Vegetable oil supplies bulk of the fat and keeps the lard from burning at the frying stage. Use both. Lard alone tends to smoke off before the paste fries; vegetable oil alone reads flat. This is analogous to the butter + olive oil split in the halfway-pesto pasta sauce — one for character, one for volume.

A MIX OF MEATS BEATS ONE ALONE (GROUND MEAT PATH): in the ground meat path, a mix of two reads better than one type alone. The tested combination is ground turkey + ground beef (or ground turkey + ground vegan meat) — one carries fat-and-savory while the other adds textural variety. Avoids the monotony of a single ground-meat texture. This applies to the ground meat path only; the chunky beef path is a single-meat dish by design.

TOMATOES ARE CLASHING (WHOLE FRESH OR CANNED) — TOMATO PASTE ONLY: previously fresh tomato was allowed as a microdose. That's out. Fresh whole tomatoes and canned tomatoes both pull the dish into american chili territory and clash here. Tomato paste is the ONLY acceptable tomato form (see DEPTH REQUIREMENT above — and remember worcestershire is the alternative to tomato paste, not an addition-on-top).

CHOCOLATE — CORRECTION STEP FOR FRUITINESS: a microdose of unsweetened chocolate (or a square of dark) damps fruity/candied character in the sauce. Primary use: when the guajillo is doing too much (either you tilted the chile ratio guajillo-heavy or you used too much tomato paste — both push the sauce fruit-forward), the chocolate's bitterness corrects it. Pinch of sugar alongside if using unsweetened, to keep it from reading austere. Chocolate is for nuance, not body — reach for masa harina/jalapeños/garlic first.

STOCK — BEEF STOCK PRIMARY, CHICKEN OR VEGETABLE SECONDARY: beef stock is the right primary regardless of which meat path you pick. It carries the deep-red-meat register the dish wants and reinforces the chile-and-masa-harina backbone. Chicken stock works as a substitute and doesn't ruin the dish, but reads lighter. Vegetable stock also works but reads lightest of the three. Use beef stock if you have it.

FAILURE MODE — TOO MUCH BOUILLON / STOCK: chile colorado reduces further than a stew, and the sauce concentrates hard at the end. Adding more bouillon or stock mid-cook to fix early-stage bland broth is a trap — that extra salt/umami over-concentrates as the liquid cooks off and the final dish reads salty or metallic. Fix early blandness with more jalapeños, more masa harina, more garlic — not more stock.
